messages.content: what are the top ten biggest human proteins?

The top ten biggest human proteins, based on their length in amino acids, are:

1. Titin (also known as connectin)
2. Nebulin
3. Obscurin
4. Dysferlin
5. TTN-AS1 (a non-coding RNA associated with Titin)
6. Dystrophin
7. X-linked myotubular myopathy 1 (also known as MTM1)
8. Titin-cap
9. Lamin A/C
10. RNA polymerase II subunit A (also known as POLR2A)

Please note that the ranking may vary depending on the exact definition and criteria used in measuring protein length.
please add number of amino acids for all of these and make it table view

Certainly! Here is a table view providing the top ten biggest human proteins along with their respective number of amino acids:

| No. | Protein Name                                 | Number of Amino Acids |
| --- | -------------------------------------------- | --------------------- |
| 1   | Titin (Connectin)                            | ~38,000               |
| 2   | Nebulin                                      | ~34,350               |
| 3   | Obscurin                                     | ~34,150               |
| 4   | Dysferlin                                    | ~2,175                |
| 5   | TTN-AS1 (Non-coding RNA associated with Titin) | ~1,680                |
| 6   | Dystrophin                                   | ~3,685                |
| 7   | X-linked myotubular myopathy 1 (MTM1)         | ~1,580                |
| 8   | Titin-cap                                    | ~1,300                |
| 9   | Lamin A/C                                    | ~647                  |
| 10  | RNA polymerase II subunit A (POLR2A)          | ~1,964                |

Note: The number of amino acids mentioned is approximate and may vary slightly depending on the specific isoforms or variants of the protein.
add short (two to five words) description of where in body it is used, for each one

Certainly! Here is an updated table with a short description of where each of the top ten biggest human proteins is used in the body:

| No. | Protein Name                                 | Number of Amino Acids | Description                                         |
| --- | -------------------------------------------- | --------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- |
| 1   | Titin (Connectin)                            | ~38,000               | Muscle structure and elasticity                     |
| 2   | Nebulin                                      | ~34,350               | Muscle structure and regulation                     |
| 3   | Obscurin                                     | ~34,150               | Muscle structure and signaling                      |
| 4   | Dysferlin                                    | ~2,175                | Muscle membrane repair                              |
| 5   | TTN-AS1 (Non-coding RNA associated with Titin) | ~1,680                | Regulation of Titin expression                      |
| 6   | Dystrophin                                   | ~3,685                | Muscle membrane stability (absence causes muscular dystrophy) |
| 7   | X-linked myotubular myopathy 1 (MTM1)         | ~1,580                | Regulation of muscle structure and function         |
| 8   | Titin-cap                                    | ~1,300                | Connects Titin to muscle Z-disc                     |
| 9   | Lamin A/C                                    | ~647                  | Nucleus structure and gene regulation               |
| 10  | RNA polymerase II subunit A (POLR2A)          | ~1,964                | Transcription of protein-coding genes               |

Please note that these are simplified descriptions and each protein may have additional functions within the body.
make same table for top ten shortest human proteins

Here is a table providing the top ten shortest human proteins along with their respective number of amino acids and a brief description:

| No. | Protein Name                             | Number of Amino Acids | Description                                          |
| --- | ---------------------------------------- | --------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| 1   | Tripeptidyl peptidase 1 (TPP1)            | 33                    | Enzyme involved in lysosomal protein degradation      |
| 2   | Gamma-synuclein                           | 79                    | Roles in synaptic function and neurodegenerative diseases |
| 3   | Urocortin-3                               | 126                   | Regulates stress response and cardiovascular function |
| 4   | Neuropeptide FF-amide peptide precursor    | 133                   | Involved in pain modulation and reproduction         |
| 5   | Leptin                                    | 167                   | Hormone regulating appetite and energy balance        |
| 6   | Insulin-like growth factor II (IGF-II)     | 180                   | Regulates growth and development                      |
| 7   | Carbonic anhydrase VII                     | 204                   | Catalyzes carbon dioxide hydration                   |
| 8   | Gastrin-releasing peptide                  | 107                   | Regulates gastric acid secretion                      |
| 9   | Prolactin-releasing peptide                | 84                    | Regulates prolactin release and reproductive behaviors |
| 10  | Platelet factor 4                          | 70                    | Involved in blood coagulation and inflammation        |

Please note that while these proteins are shorter in length, they still play crucial roles in various physiological processes within the human body.

**Топ-10 самых коротких человеческих белков:**

| 2  | Гамма-синуклеин                            | 79                    | Роли в синаптической функции и нейродегенеративных заболеваниях           |
| 3  | Урокортин-3                                | 126                   | Регуляция стресс-ответа и сердечно-сосудистой функции                    |
| 4  | Прекурсор пептида нейропептида FF           | 133                   | Участвует в модуляции боли и репродукции                                 |

Обратите внимание, что эти белки являются более короткими по длине, но они все же играют важные роли в различных физиологических процессах в человеческом организме.
what is the biggest known protein in living organisms?

The largest known protein in living organisms is called titin (connectin). It is a muscle protein found in vertebrates, including humans. Titin is known for its enormous size, with its gene encoding the protein being one of the largest known. In humans, this protein can consist of over 38,000 amino acids, making it the largest known protein both in terms of length and molecular weight. Titin plays a crucial role in muscle structure and elasticity, contributing to the proper functioning and elasticity of skeletal muscle fibers.
translate this to russian also

Самый большой известный белок в живых организмах называется титин (коннектин). Он является мышечным белком, обнаруженным у позвоночных животных, включая человека. Титин известен своим гигантским размером, и ген, кодирующий этот белок, считается одним из самых крупных. У человека этот белок может состоять из более чем 38 000 аминокислот, что делает его самым большим известным белком как по длине, так и по молекулярной массе. Титин играет важную роль в структуре мышц и их эластичности, способствуя правильному функционированию и эластичности скелетных мышечных волокон.
please provide links to sources for both tables
